preamble: Diphthongization of the close-mid front unrounded vowel [e] to the diphthong [eo] through vowel breaking and glide insertion. This shift occurs in stressed syllables in Old English, Romance languages, and related Germanic languages, representing a systematic pattern of vowel breaking in specific phonological environments.

examples.note: In Old English vowel breaking, /e/ became /eo/ when followed by velar consonants /x/, /w/, or by /r/, /l/ plus consonant clusters, creating systematic diphthongization patterns.
In Romance vowel breaking patterns, stressed [e] develops into [eo] in certain phonological contexts, particularly in open syllables alongside other vowel diphthongizations.
Historical Spanish shows diphthongization of stressed [e] into [eo] in specific contexts, exemplifying broader Romance vowel breaking processes.
